text: The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic mini gastric bypass by inserting a varus needle at the inferior crease of the umbilicus. A supraumbilical port is established for the telescope, while additional ports are placed at the left epigastric region for the stapler, left and right hypochondrium, and left anterior axillary line, following the standard port placement for this procedure.

The assistant lifts the abdominal wall as the surgeon introduces a 20 cm Veress needle perpendicular to the surface, pointing towards the anus. The surgeon performs irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm needle placement. A Striker microprocessor insufflator in bariatric mode is used to insufflate the abdomen with approximately 4.5 liters of gas, reaching a pressure of 15 mmHg. The supraumbilical port is prepared for telescope insertion.

The surgeon introduces a 10mm port for the telescope, allowing inspection of the abdominal cavity. Insufflator tubing is attached, increasing the flow rate to 20 liters per minute. Adhesions of the omentum are observed near the gallbladder area in the right hypochondrium. The right hypochondriac port is designated for the stapler, while the left hypochondriac port is used for the surgeon's right hand during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the epigastric port to lift the omentum, while the right hypochondriac port holds a lever retractor. The left hypochondriac port is used with the ligature in the right hand. An omental window is created at the level of the second gastric pedicle, which is then enlarged to inspect for any adhesions below the stomach.

The surgeon uses a ligature to create a window in the tissue, revealing minimal adhesions. Through the epigastric port, a stapler is used to create a horizontal staple line for forming the gastric tube. The first staple cartridge is fired, and the surgeon ensures no adhesions are present in the retrogastric area before continuing with the vertical staple line. Adhesions are separated using harmonic or ligature as needed. After firing the second staple, the stomach is lifted with a grasper to inspect the retrogastric area, confirming minimal adhesions. The third staple cartridge is introduced, and any remaining adhesions are addressed with harmonic dissection.

The surgeon introduces the third staple cartridge near the gastric pouch, aiming for precise placement to optimize the restrictive component of the mini gastric bypass. The grasper lifts the stomach to inspect the posterior gastric area for adhesions. The telescope provides a view of the retro gastric area, where the ligature is used for adhesiolysis. Careful dissection is performed to separate any adhesions involving the spleen or retro gastric fat cushion, using blunt dissection to reach the diaphragm.

The surgeon carefully lifts the stomach with the left hand while using the right hand to guide the telescope into the retrogastric tunnel. The shiny fascia of the diaphragm becomes visible as the blunt dissection progresses. A window is created near the diaphragm, and the surgeon applies a 60 mm stapler cartridge to continue the gastric bypass procedure. The stapler cuts through the tissue, reaching up to the fundus, with a small portion of the fundus tag remaining attached at the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon removes the previous cartridge and introduces a new one to cut a small section of tissue. After ensuring no bleeding, suction irrigation is performed. The omentum is lifted, and the small bowel is manipulated to reach the first part of the jejunum near the ligament of Treitz. The surgeon confirms the location by visualizing the ligament and ensuring the transverse colon and greater omentum are lifted.
The surgeon measures and marks the jejunum, ensuring it is positioned without torsion, as they count the length in increments up to forty-one centimeters, keeping the jejunum towards the left side.

The surgeon counts up to fifty, indicating the measurement of 2.5 meters of intestine. A single anastomosis is performed for the mini gastric bypass, ensuring no rotation occurs. A window is created in the stomach above the staple line on the anterior wall using a harmonic device. The gastric calibration tube is inserted into the stomach, and the assistant pulls it in, taking care to avoid forming a subserosal pocket.

The surgeon cuts and perforates the jejunum, ensuring the mucosa is visible. Graspers are introduced, and a stapler is used to perform a side-to-side anastomosis between the jejunum and stomach using a 60mm cartridge. Both edges are aligned evenly to maintain blood supply before firing the stapler. After firing, the anastomosis is completed, and the stapler is removed. Suction is applied to lift the tissue, confirming a well-formed anastomosis with the buzi and jejunal lumen visible.

The surgeon begins closing the suture line using a barb suture, performing a two-layer closure. The first layer is approximated, followed by the serosal layer. The barb suture, with laser cuts every millimeter, provides self-holding properties, but the surgeon ties a knot for added security. A surgeon's knot is tied using a double wrap followed by alternating wraps, ensuring the closure is secure.

The surgeon uses a pre-tied loop to start the knot and continues closing the staple hole between the jejunum and the stomach line using a barbed suture made of polygalactane. The left hand instrument lifts the falciform ligament slightly while taking continuous edge-to-edge bites.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing along the posterior aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring the corners are adequately sutured to prevent leaks. The mucosa is inverted to avoid occlusion of the lumen, with the left hand supporting the suture and the right hand taking bites. Care is taken to maintain appropriate tension without excessive pulling to prevent cut-through.

The surgeon interlocks the sutures at the corners of the anastomosis, ensuring they are closed adequately. The suturing continues beyond the corners to reach the healthy margin, emphasizing the importance of securing these areas with locking stitches.

The surgeon interlocks the suture at the healthy margin to ensure proper hold, lifting the tissue for inspection. A bite is taken on the margin, and the suture is continued from serosa to serosa for a second layer using a self-retaining barb suture, which does not require immediate termination.
The surgeon carefully retracts the tissue to reinforce the first staple line, focusing on the zero muscular layer.

The surgeon carefully embeds and buries the corners of the suture line, ensuring the integrity of the seromuscular layer while closing the staple hole completely. Methylene blue is used to check for leaks, with 100 ml applied to confirm the closure's effectiveness.

The surgeon ties a knot using a double wrap followed by a single wrap to terminate the suture, then cuts the suture with scissors. Two graspers hold either side of the lumen, occluding it with a long jaw clasp. Methylene blue dye is introduced, visibly distending the lumen without any leakage, confirming the integrity of the suture line.

The surgeon checks for leaks using methylene blue dye, confirming no leakage. A suture is placed on the serosa, and the afferent limb is fixed to the gastric tube using a white reel, ensuring it is parallel and tension-free to relieve the staple line. The fixation is secured with a surgeon's knot.

The surgeon ties a knot to secure the suture, minimizing tension on the staple line. The suture is then cut, and another loop is fixed to a part of the stomach, with the knot tied to secure it in place.

The surgeon performs a C and reverse C knot to release tension from the jejunum over the staple line. The omentum is pulled to cover the entire anastomotic line of the mini gastric bypass and is fixed to the stomach using a titanium clip or knot.
